narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 55.8% chance of not passing. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades, where you landed in the 1st decile at school, the single strongest signal pulling toward failure because it suggested the rigors of advanced legal coursework might prove especially tough. That worry built on your Year 1 decile of 3rd place, another key factor reinforcing that early hurdles could snowball. Your LSAT score of 38 added to the concern, as it positioned you below the levels that typically light the way to bar success for students like you. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket played a role too, hinting at fewer resources for the intense prep that often makes the difference. Your background as a Black female applicant tugged in the same direction, reflecting patterns where systemic barriers have historically stacked the odds. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 2.6 and full-time study commitment, while positive in spirit, weren't quite enough to counterbalance when layered with your race and the rest. Together, these pieces painted a picture of a determined student facing an uphill battle against too many headwinds at once. But here's the empowering part—this isn't your full story yet; it's just a data-driven snapshot urging you to target those weak spots, like boosting test prep or seeking extra support, before reapplying stronger. You've got the drive, and with focused changes, you can rewrite this narrative. We're rooting for you.
